critic_raw:
The stratified random split on patient visit sequences is the primary structural flaw. The same patient's visits across multiple hospital stays can span training and test splits. For an RNN trained on visit sequences, seeing earlier visits from a patient in training and predicting on a later visit in test constitutes within-patient leakage — the model can learn patient-specific physiological baselines, coding patterns, and readmission propensity directly from training observations. The correct split unit is patient, not visit.

The claim that 'temporal autocorrelation effects from hospital practices are weak' is a post-hoc rationalization for a methodologically convenient choice. Hospital readmission rates are documented to be affected by policy changes, seasonal flu burden, staff patterns, and quality improvement programs — all of which create temporal structure. The assumption is stated as a design choice rather than verified empirically.

The comparison gives the RNN sequential features (temporally encoded visit sequences) and gives logistic regression aggregate features (demographic and diagnostic summaries). This is not an architectural comparison — it is a feature richness comparison. Any RNN advantage is uninterpretable without a baseline that uses the same features (e.g., a bag-of-visit-codes logistic regression or a mean-pooled embedding).

Stratification 'by hospital' for splitting is mentioned but not reconciled with the stratified random split methodology — if splits are stratified by hospital, do patients from the same hospital appear in all splits, or is each hospital assigned to one split? The former permits hospital-specific leakage; the latter is a site-based split, which is not what was described.
all_issues_raised: The stratified random split does not use patient as the split unit — the same patient's multiple visits can appear in both training and test, allowing the RNN to learn patient-specific readmission propensity from training observations and apply it at test time.

The 'weak temporal autocorrelation' assumption is asserted rather than verified — hospital readmission rates are known to vary with policy changes, seasonal illness burden, and quality improvement programs, all of which create temporal structure that a random split obscures.

The RNN receives sequential visit features while logistic regression receives aggregate features — the comparison is not architectural but rather reflects a difference in feature richness, making any observed advantage unattributable to the sequence modeling mechanism.

The relationship between hospital-level stratification and the stated stratified random split is ambiguous — if patients from the same hospital appear across all splits, hospital-specific leakage occurs; if hospitals are assigned to single splits, this becomes a site-based split with dramatically different statistical properties than described.
all_issues_adjudicated: The stratified random split does not use patient as the split unit — the same patient's multiple visits can appear in both training and test, allowing the RNN to learn patient-specific readmission propensity from training observations and apply it at test time.

The 'weak temporal autocorrelation' assumption is asserted rather than verified — hospital readmission rates are known to vary with policy changes, seasonal illness burden, and quality improvement programs, all of which create temporal structure that a random split obscures.

The RNN receives sequential visit features while logistic regression receives aggregate features — the comparison is not architectural but rather reflects a difference in feature richness, making any observed advantage unattributable to the sequence modeling mechanism.

The relationship between hospital-level stratification and the stated stratified random split is ambiguous — if patients from the same hospital appear across all splits, hospital-specific leakage occurs; if hospitals are assigned to single splits, this becomes a site-based split with dramatically different statistical properties than described.
